name: Minnesota
tagline: A new state CIO, a sweeping data-center tax incentive driving a hyperscale buildout, and a whole-of-state cybersecurity push — Minnesota's technology agenda is in motion mid-biennium.

execSummary.summary: Minnesota runs on a two-year (biennial) cycle; the FY2026-27 budget signed by Governor Walz in June 2025 holds net general-fund spending to roughly $66B, so the live opportunity is mid-biennium execution rather than a fresh appropriation. Three shifts matter to a Dell seller. First, leadership turned over: Jon Eichten became MNIT Commissioner and State CIO on March 16, 2026, succeeding longtime CIO Tarek Tomes, who moved to a senior technology role at the University of Minnesota — putting a Dell-relevant buyer on both sides of that move. Second, a 2025 data-center tax law (Chapter 13) added a 'qualified large-scale data center' tier and extended the equipment sales-tax exemption to 35 years, helping pull a wave of hyperscale projects (Meta's ~$800M Rosemount campus is under construction; 20-plus more proposed) — though the state simultaneously ended the electricity exemption and the boom faces grid and local-opposition headwinds. Third, MNIT is running a Whole-of-State Cybersecurity Plan and a TAIGA AI-governance effort, both creating governed infrastructure and security demand. Buying runs through MNIT Procurement with the Department of Administration's Office of State Procurement, and cooperatively through the Cooperative Purchasing Venture (CPV).
opportunities: AI-infrastructure / 'AI factory' demand tied to Minnesota's hyperscale data-center buildout (PowerEdge, networking, services) and to private buyers landing in-state.
Whole-of-State Cybersecurity Plan rollout to state and local government — secure compute, endpoint, and managed-security services for under-resourced counties, cities, and schools.
Governed AI under MNIT's TAIGA framework — an opening for private/on-prem AI on sensitive state data rather than open public models.
Health-tech and research-computing pull from Mayo Clinic's $5B 'Bold. Forward. Unbound.' Rochester build and the University of Minnesota's research mission.
Endpoint, client, and lifecycle refresh across executive-branch agencies funded through MNIT's chargeback model.

budget.intro: Minnesota budgets on a two-year (biennial) cycle. The FY2026-27 budget was passed by the Legislature and signed by Governor Walz in June 2025, holding net general-fund spending to just under $66B for the biennium. There is no single 'state IT budget' line: MNIT, the central IT agency, is funded almost entirely through a chargeback model in which agencies pay for the services they consume, with only about 5% coming from direct general-fund and special-revenue appropriations. Technology spend is therefore distributed across agency budgets and a set of named technology, cyber, and broadband programs.
notes: General-fund figure is a biennial (two-year) total; most technology spend sits inside agency budgets rather than a central IT line.
MNIT's chargeback model means agency demand — not a single appropriation — drives most enterprise technology buying.

2026 supplemental enacted: the Legislature adjourned May 17, 2026 and Walz signed a bipartisan end-of-session package (incl. a ~$1.2B bonding/infrastructure bill). Net +$63M general-fund change for the FY2026-27 biennium leaves a projected ~$3.67B balance; a balanced-budget posture that slows program growth while funding multi-year legacy-IT modernization.

initiatives.name: Hyperscale Data-Center Buildout
initiatives.summary: A 2025 law (Chapter 13) created a 'qualified large-scale data center' tier (25,000+ sq ft and $250M+ invested within 60 months) and extended the IT-equipment sales-tax exemption to 35 years, pulling a wave of hyperscale projects. Meta's ~$800M Rosemount campus is under construction and slated to open later in 2026. Proposed statewide regulations/moratorium efforts stalled at the Capitol in 2026 (the Legislature adjourned without new restrictions, halted ~June 12 after union/industry pushback), so the favorable tax framework stands — but local opposition keeps spreading via city/county moratoria (Minneapolis 6-mo, Rosemount/Lonsdale/Inver Grove Heights 1-yr, Nobles County rezoning denial). Still the strongest tailwind for Dell AI-infrastructure demand in Minnesota — but siting risk is now a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction question; qualify the pipeline locally.

initiatives.name: Whole-of-State Cybersecurity Plan
initiatives.summary: MNIT, with the Minnesota Cybersecurity Task Force, is distributing $23.5M (federal SLCGP/SSMI plus state match) to harden state and local cyber defenses, with funding steered toward rural and under-resourced jurisdictions. Counties, cities, and school districts gaining grant dollars but lacking staff are candidates for secure compute, endpoint, and managed-security offerings.

initiatives.name: TAIGA — AI Governance Framework
initiatives.summary: MNIT leads the Transparent Artificial Intelligence Governance Alliance (TAIGA) to set ethical AI guidelines for state government, having hired Minnesota's first state AI Director and stood up an AI Leads working group. As agencies operationalize governed AI, there is an opening for private/on-prem AI on sensitive data rather than open public models.

initiatives.name: Border-to-Border Broadband
initiatives.summary: The Office of Broadband Development (in DEED) runs the Border-to-Border grant program — recent biennia funded around $50M per year plus an ~$83M federal Capital Projects Fund allocation — to reach unserved and underserved areas. Primarily a middle-/last-mile infrastructure play, it signals continued state investment in the connectivity layer that downstream compute and edge needs ride on.

initiatives.name: Mayo Clinic 'Bold. Forward. Unbound.'
initiatives.summary: Mayo Clinic's ~$5B, six-year Rochester transformation blends physical and digital infrastructure with integrated telehealth, digital health, AI, robotics, and a new logistics center. Though Mayo is a private system rather than a state agency, it is a marquee Minnesota health-tech and research-computing buyer with strong Dell relevance across compute, storage, and AI.

procurement.intro: Minnesota does not centralize IT buying in a single contract authority the way some states do. MNIT's Procurement Division works with the Department of Administration's Office of State Procurement (OSP) to establish competitive vendor contracts for IT hardware, software, and professional services for executive-branch agencies. Many of those contracts are also extended to other public entities through the state's Cooperative Purchasing Venture (CPV).

vehicles.name: MNIT / OSP IT contracts
vehicles.detail: Competitively established contracts for IT hardware, software, and professional services for executive-branch agencies. Confirm Dell agreements and resellers are current and correctly scoped.

vehicles.name: Cooperative Purchasing Venture (CPV)
vehicles.detail: Extends eligible state contracts to cities, counties, school districts, and other public entities — a path to local-government and education buyers.

vehicles.name: National cooperatives (e.g., NASPO ValuePoint)
vehicles.detail: Minnesota participates in national cooperative agreements that public buyers also use; verify which vehicle a given account prefers before pursuing a deal.

entities.name: MNIT
entities.role: Central IT agency: enterprise services, security, AI governance, and IT procurement coordination. Primary statewide technology relationship.

entities.name: Dept. of Administration — Office of State Procurement
entities.role: Establishes and administers state contracts, including the CPV cooperative path.

entities.name: University of Minnesota
entities.role: Large, independent research-computing and AI-infrastructure buyer; now home to former state CIO Tarek Tomes.

entities.name: Counties, cities & school districts
entities.role: Whole-of-State cyber grant recipients buying through CPV; often staff-constrained and services-receptive.

procurement.competition: Minnesota is a contract-driven, relationship-heavy market where incumbents hold state and cooperative agreements across compute, networking, and services. Differentiation comes from AI-infrastructure capability tied to the data-center and Mayo/UMN buildouts, governed-AI and security offerings that map to TAIGA and the Whole-of-State plan, and managed services for under-resourced local governments — rather than price alone.

powerStructure.name: Tim Walz
powerStructure.title: Governor
powerStructure.note: Signed the FY2026-27 budget and the 2025 data-center tax law. Withdrew from the 2026 reelection race (Jan 5, 2026) but remains in office through Jan 2027 — a lame-duck posture. Sen. Amy Klobuchar won the DFL endorsement (May 30) to succeed him; GOP field led by House Speaker Lisa Demuth (primary Aug 11, general Nov 3). Build relationships with MNIT career leadership and prepare for a possible Jan 2027 cabinet refresh.

powerStructure.name: Jon Eichten
powerStructure.title: Commissioner & State CIO
powerStructure.note: Appointed March 16, 2026; MNIT leader since 2012 (most recently Deputy Commissioner). Confirmed permanent and current as of June 2026. Owns statewide tech direction.

powerStructure.name: John Israel
powerStructure.title: Assistant Commissioner & State CISO
powerStructure.note: Permanent CISO since 2023 (with MNIT since 2008); leads executive-branch cybersecurity and the Minnesota Cybersecurity Task Force / Whole-of-State plan. Primary security buyer.

powerStructure.name: Brandon Hirsch
powerStructure.title: Deputy Commissioner
powerStructure.note: Current MNIT Deputy Commissioner (succeeding Eichten, who moved up to Commissioner). Oversees enterprise business operations and agency partner relationships.

powerStructure.name: Tarek Tomes
powerStructure.title: VP for Information Technology & CIO
powerStructure.note: Led MNIT 2019-2026; became U of M's VP for IT and CIO effective Mar 16, 2026 — a warm, Dell-relevant relationship on the higher-ed side.

powerStructure.name: Office of State Procurement
powerStructure.title: State contracting authority
powerStructure.note: Establishes IT contracts and the CPV cooperative path used by state and local buyers.

powerStructure.name: Minnesota Cybersecurity Task Force
powerStructure.title: Whole-of-state cyber governance
powerStructure.note: Steers SLCGP/SSMI grant priorities to state and local jurisdictions.

news.title: Statewide data-center regulations stall at Capitol; new local moratoria continue
news.impact: Proposed statewide rules on hyperscale data centers were halted (~June 12) after pushback from labor unions and industry; the Legislature adjourned without new restrictions, so the Chapter 13 tax framework stands. Local moratoria keep spreading (Lonsdale in May; Inver Grove Heights in late June) alongside the earlier Minneapolis/Rosemount actions. Net effect: state-level tailwind for hyperscale remains intact, but siting risk is now a city/county-by-jurisdiction question — qualify pipeline locally.

news.title: Klobuchar wins DFL gubernatorial endorsement; open-seat race takes shape
news.impact: U.S. Sen. Amy Klobuchar was endorsed by the DFL on the first ballot (May 30) to succeed lame-duck Gov. Walz; GOP field led by House Speaker Lisa Demuth. Primary Aug 11, general Nov 3, 2026. A new administration takes office Jan 2027 — anchor relationships with MNIT career leadership now and prepare for a possible cabinet refresh.

news.title: Walz signs 2026 supplemental budget; session ends May 17
news.impact: Bipartisan end-of-session deal: ~$1.2B bonding/infrastructure bill, $205M one-time for HCMC, $250M car-tab relief, $125M property-tax relief. Net +$63M GF leaves a projected ~$3.67B balance; funds multi-year legacy-IT modernization and a Governor's Council on the Future AI Economy.

news.title: Jon Eichten named permanent MNIT Commissioner & State CIO
news.impact: Tech direction stabilizes under a long-tenured MNIT leader (made permanent Mar 16); re-confirm relationships and priorities with the new CIO's office.

news.title: Tarek Tomes becomes University of Minnesota VP for IT & CIO
news.impact: Former state CIO takes the U of M's top tech seat (effective Mar 16) — a warm, Dell-relevant higher-ed relationship to cultivate.

news.title: Mayo 'Bold. Forward. Unbound.' Rochester build advances
news.impact: Marquee ~$5B health-tech program with AI, robotics, and digital infrastructure — a strong private-sector Dell account.

dellActions: Lead the AI-infrastructure conversation: tie Dell ISG / AI Factory offerings to Minnesota's hyperscale data-center buildout, Mayo's Rochester program, and University of Minnesota research computing.
Re-engage MNIT under new CIO Jon Eichten to re-confirm priorities, and leverage the warm University of Minnesota relationship via former state CIO Tarek Tomes.
Target Whole-of-State cyber grant recipients — counties, cities, and school districts — with secure compute, endpoint, and managed-security offerings sized for staff-constrained teams.
Position governed, private/on-prem AI for sensitive workloads as agencies operationalize MNIT's TAIGA framework.
Validate Dell's coverage on MNIT/OSP IT contracts and the CPV cooperative path so state and local buyers can transact without friction.
